reasoning:
Caen (France) has an oceanic climate. The exact daily average temperature on our baseline date, Oct 26 ($T_0$), is unknown, so we treat it as a random variable with mean ~11.5°C and standard deviation ~3.5°C. For the target dates:
- Nov 2 ($T_1$): Mean ~10.5°C. Expecting a small seasonal drop. Difference $T_1 - T_0$ has mean ~ -1.0°C. Standard deviation of the difference is ~3.5-4.0°C (accounting for short-term autocorrelation). $P(T_1 > T_0) \approx 0.39$.
- Nov 25 ($T_2$): Mean ~7.5°C. Uncorrelated with $T_0$. Difference mean ~ -4.0°C. SD of difference ~5.3°C. $P(T_2 > T_0) \approx 0.24$.
- Jan 24 ($T_3$): Mean ~5.3°C. Difference mean ~ -6.2°C. SD of difference ~ 5.7°C. $P(T_3 > T_0) \approx 0.16$. 
The uncertainty (variance of the difference) increases over time, but the strong downward seasonal trend in the Northern Hemisphere heavily dominates, shifting the probability mass further away from 0.5. To account for heavy-tailed temperature distributions, the probabilities are slightly moderated.
